critique.summary: The article explains the differences between Linux's compressed swap technologies zswap and zram, recommending zswap for most use cases due to its tighter integration with the kernel's memory management and automatic tiering. It highlights zram's limitations, such as lack of automatic eviction and potential performance issues, making it suitable mainly for embedded or diskless systems with specific constraints.
mainPoints: zswap acts as a compression layer in front of disk swap, automatically evicting cold pages to disk and integrating with the kernel's memory management.
zram creates a compressed block device in RAM with a fixed capacity and no automatic eviction, which can lead to LRU inversion and system hangs under pressure.
Using zram alongside disk swap can degrade performance by filling fast RAM with cold pages and pushing active pages to slow disk.
zswap handles incompressible data better by rejecting it and sending it directly to disk, while zram compresses all data regardless of compressibility.
zswap reduces SSD wear by absorbing frequent page-out/page-in operations in RAM, whereas zram can increase disk I/O by forcing more page cache eviction.
Fedora uses a zram-only approach with systemd-oomd to avoid disk swap for security and complexity reasons, but this setup requires careful management to avoid system hangs.
Future kernel development favors zswap as the single compressed swap implementation, potentially with backing-store-less operation, reducing the need for zram.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed, technically accurate explanation of zswap and zram based on kernel internals and real-world experience, including references to kernel versions, code snippets, and production use cases. The claims about zswap's advantages in integration, automatic tiering, and handling of memory pressure are consistent with Linux kernel documentation and expert commentary. The discussion of zram's limitations, such as lack of automatic eviction and LRU inversion, aligns with known issues reported by kernel developers and users. The article also correctly notes Fedora's rationale for using zram-only setups and the ongoing kernel development trends favoring zswap. Overall, the information is well-supported and credible.
considerations: The article focuses primarily on Linux kernel memory management and may not cover all use cases or hardware configurations where zram could be beneficial.
Performance and behavior can vary depending on workload characteristics, kernel versions, and system tuning, so empirical testing is recommended for specific environments.
Security considerations around swap data on persistent storage versus RAM are important but only briefly touched on.
The article assumes familiarity with Linux kernel internals and may be challenging for less technical readers.
The impact of newer kernel features or alternative compressed swap solutions beyond zswap and zram is not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the trade-offs between zswap and zram for swap management in Linux systems, with contributors highlighting performance, hardware compatibility, and configuration complexity. Some emphasize the importance of hardware characteristics like SSD type, while others discuss kernel features and compression algorithms influencing effectiveness.

comments.author: patrakov
comments.text:
User here, who also acts as a Level 2 support for storage.

The article contains some solid logic plus an assumption that I disagree with.

Solid logic: you should prefer zswap if you have a device that can be used for swap.

Solid logic: zram + other swap = bad due to LRU inversion (zram becomes a dead weight in memory).

Advice that matches my observations: zram works best when paired with a user-space OOM killer.

Bold assumption: everybody who has an SSD has a device that can be used for swap.

The assumption is simply false, and not due to the "SSD wear" argument. Many consumer SSDs, especially DRAMless ones (e.g., Apacer AS350 1TB, but also seen on Crucial SSDs), under synchronous writes, will regularly produce latency spikes of 10 seconds or more, due to the way they need to manage their cells. This is much worse than any HDD. If a DRAMless consumer SSD is all that you have, better use zram.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced critique based on real hardware behavior, challenging assumptions with concrete examples, making it highly credible and insightful.

comments.author: quapster
comments.text:
The interesting meta-point here is how a kernel mechanism turned into cargo-cult tuning advice.

"Use zram, save your SSD" made sense in the era of tiny eMMC, no TRIM, and mystery flash controllers. It also fit a very human bias: disk I/O feels scary and finite, CPU cycles feel free and infinite. So zram became a kind of talisman you enable once and never think about again.

But the kernel isn't optimizing for your feelings about SSD wear, it's optimizing for global memory pressure. zswap fits into that feedback loop, zram mostly sits outside it. Once you see that, the behavior people complain about ("my system thrashes and then dies mysteriously") stops being mysterious: they effectively built a second, opaque memory pool that the MM subsystem can't reason about or reclaim from cleanly.

What's funny is that on modern desktops and servers, the alleged downside of zswap (writing to disk sometimes) is the one thing the hardware is extremely good at, while the downside of zram (locking cold garbage in RAM and confusing reclaim/oom) is exactly what you don't want when the machine is under stress. The folk wisdom never updated, but the hardware and the kernel did.
comments.analysis: Offers a deep understanding of kernel memory management and explains why zswap is generally preferable on modern hardware, providing valuable context.

comments.author: MaxCompression
comments.text:
One underappreciated aspect of zswap vs zram is the compression algorithm choice and its interaction with the data being compressed.

LZ4 (default in both) is optimized for speed at the expense of ratio — typically 2-2.5x on memory pages. zstd can push that to 3-3.5x but at significantly higher CPU cost per page fault.

The interesting tradeoff: memory pages are fundamentally different from files. They contain lots of pointer-sized values, stack frames, and heap metadata — data patterns where simple LZ variants actually perform surprisingly well relative to more complex algorithms. Going beyond zstd (e.g., BWT-based or context mixing) would give diminishing returns on memory pages while destroying latency.

So the real question isn't just "zswap vs zram" but "how much CPU are you willing to spend per compressed page, given your workload's memory access patterns?" For latency-sensitive workloads, LZ4 with zswap writeback is hard to beat.
comments.analysis: Provides a technical perspective on compression trade-offs relevant to zswap and zram, enhancing understanding of performance considerations.

comments.author: garaetjjte
comments.text:
>They size the zram device to 100% of your physical RAM, capped at 8GB. You may be wondering how that makes any sense at all – how can one have a swap device that's potentially the entire size of one's RAM?

zram size applies to uncompressed data, real usage is dynamically growing (plus static bookkeeping). Most memory compresses well, so you probably want to have zram device size even larger than physical RAM.
comments.analysis: Clarifies a common misunderstanding about zram sizing, contributing useful practical knowledge.

comments.author: astrobe_
comments.text:
> It only really makes sense for extremely memory-constrained embedded systems

Even "mildly" memory constrained embedded systems don't use swap because their resources are tailored for their function. And they are typically not fans [1] of compression either because the compression rate is often unpredictable.

[1] Yes, they typically don't need fans because overheating and using a motor for cooling is a double waste of energy.
comments.analysis: Accurately describes embedded system constraints, adding relevant context to the discussion about swap usage.

comments.author: seba_dos1
comments.text:
A simpler alternative to OOM daemons could be enabling MGLRU's thrashing prevention: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/next/admin-guide/mm/multigen...

I'm using it together with zram sized to 200% RAM size on a low RAM phone with no disk swap (plus some tuning like the mentioned clustering knob) and it works pretty well if you don't mind some otherwise preventable kills, but I will happily switch to diskless zswap once it's ready.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical kernel tuning tip that complements swap strategies, showing thoughtful system-level insight.

comments.author: CoolGuySteve
comments.text:
Would be nice if zswap could be configured to have no backing cache so it could completely replace zram. Having two slightly different systems is weird.

There's not really any difference between swap on disk being full and swap in ram being full, either way something needs to get OOM killed.

Simplifying the configuration would probably also make it easier to enable by default in most distros. It's kind of backwards that the most common Linux distros other than ChromeOS are behind Mac and Windows in this regard.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid usability and configuration concern, though more speculative about future features.

comments.author: Szpadel
comments.text:
There is one more feature that zram can do: multiple compression levels. I use simple bash script to first use fast compression and after 1h recompress it using much stronger compression.

unfortunately you cannot chain it with any additional layer or offload to disk later on, because recompression breaks idle tracking by setting timestamp to 0 (so it's 1970 again)

https://gist.github.com/Szpadel/9a1960e52121e798a240a9b320ec...
comments.analysis: Shares a practical enhancement and limitation of zram, contributing useful technical detail.

comments.author: prussian
comments.text:
With zram, I can just use zram-generator[0] and it does everything for me and I don't even need to set anything up, other than installing the systemd generator, which on some distros, it's installed by default. Is there anything equivalent for zswap? Otherwise, I'm not surprised most people are just using zram, even if sub-optimal.

[0]: https://crates.io/crates/zram-generator
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of tooling and ease of configuration in user adoption, a relevant practical point.

comments.author: guenthert
comments.text: So much polemic and no numbers?  If it is a performance issue, show me the numbers!
comments.analysis: Calls for empirical data to support claims, emphasizing the need for evidence though expressed somewhat emotionally.

comments.author: tonnydourado
comments.text: That's a banger article, I don't even like low level stuff and yet read the whole thing. Hopefully I will have opportunity to use some of it if I ever get around to switch my personal notebook back to linux
comments.analysis: Expresses appreciation but adds little analytical value to the technical discussion.

comments.author: nephanth
comments.text:
I used to put swap on zram when my laptop had one of those early ssds, that people would tell you not to put swap on for fear of wearing them out

Setup was tedious
comments.analysis: Shares personal experience that aligns with historical concerns, though anecdotal and less analytical.

comments.author: jitl
comments.text: thank goodness Kubernetes got support for swap; zswap has been a great boon for one of my workloads
comments.analysis: Brief endorsement of zswap in a specific context, adding limited technical detail.

comments.author: Mashimo
comments.text: Is this advice also applicable to Desktops installations?
comments.analysis: A question without elaboration or contribution to the discussion.

comments.author: adgjlsfhk1
comments.text: can you make a follow-up here for the best way to setup swap to support full disk encryption+hybernation?
comments.analysis: A request for further content rather than a substantive comment.
